timeline.title: New Delhi, Delhi, India: Standing history in sequence
timeline.overview: A route through surviving sites, ordered from the oldest standing layer to the newest urban memory still visible today.
timeline.whyThisRouteWorks: The route keeps to sites that still exist, stay mappable on the ground, and form a coherent chronological walk through the city.
timeline.transitionLogic: Each stop advances the city through a visible change in patronage, form, or civic memory.

chapters.summary: One of the most important fortified traces of early Mughal-era Delhi and a major urban ruin still physically present.
A foundational Mughal monument and UNESCO World Heritage site that anchors the Nizamuddin historic landscape.
One of Delhi’s strongest surviving power monuments and a UNESCO World Heritage site.
Marks the transition from Mughal urban patronage to later city layers and remains a prominent protected monument.

feasibleStops.title: Purana Qila
feasibleStops.historicalSummary: A major surviving fortification complex associated with Humayun’s Dinpanah and later reworked under Sher Shah Suri.
feasibleStops.whyItMatters: One of the most important fortified traces of early Mughal-era Delhi and a major urban ruin still physically present.

feasibleStops.title: Humayun's Tomb
feasibleStops.historicalSummary: A pioneering Mughal garden-tomb commissioned for Emperor Humayun and a key precedent for later imperial funerary architecture.
feasibleStops.whyItMatters: A foundational Mughal monument and UNESCO World Heritage site that anchors the Nizamuddin historic landscape.

feasibleStops.title: Red Fort
feasibleStops.historicalSummary: Mughal imperial fort begun under Shah Jahan and preserved as a major surviving symbol of Shahjahanabad and later Indian state memory.
feasibleStops.whyItMatters: One of Delhi’s strongest surviving power monuments and a UNESCO World Heritage site.

feasibleStops.title: Safdarjung's Tomb
feasibleStops.historicalSummary: A late Mughal mausoleum in Delhi that preserves a major imperial funerary form on the eve of colonial modernity.
feasibleStops.whyItMatters: Marks the transition from Mughal urban patronage to later city layers and remains a prominent protected monument.
